title: Extract, Define, Canonicalize: An LLM-based Framework for Knowledge Graph Construction
authors: Bowen Zhang, Harold Soh (Department of Computer Science, National University of Singapore; Smart Systems Institute (SSI), National University of Singapore)
venue: Proceedings of the 2024 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP 2024), per arXiv 'Comments' metadata field (not printed inside the v1 HTML body itself; extraction is based on v1, submitted 5 Apr 2024)
claim: The paper proposes Extract-Define-Canonicalize (EDC), a three-phase LLM-based framework for knowledge graph construction (KGC) that performs open information extraction, then schema definition, then post-hoc schema canonicalization, avoiding the need to pre-load a full target schema into the prompt. EDC works both when a pre-defined target schema exists (Target Alignment) and when none is provided (Self Canonicalization), and an optional Refinement phase (EDC+R) with a trained Schema Retriever further improves extraction. The central empirical claim is that EDC (and EDC+R) extract higher-quality KGs than specialized state-of-the-art trained models (REGEN, GenIE) and outperform a clustering-based canonicalizer (CESI), while scaling to much larger schemas than prior LLM-prompting KGC methods without any parameter tuning of the base LLMs (Abstract; §1; §5 Conclusion).
novelty: Authors present the novelty as: (1) decoupling schema size from prompt length by using post-hoc canonicalization instead of requiring the full schema in-context, which prior LLM-based KGC methods (ChatIE, CodeKGC) could not do at scale (§1, §2); (2) generality across both a pre-defined-schema setting (Target Alignment) and a no-schema setting (Self Canonicalization) within one framework, versus prior canonicalization work that is specialized to one or the other (Putri et al. 2019 for alignment; CESI/Vashishth et al. 2018 for clustering) (§2, §3.1); (3) using LLM-generated natural-language schema definitions plus vector similarity as 'contextual and semantically-rich side information' instead of static external resources like WordNet/PPDB, and using LLM verification of each proposed transformation to counter clustering methods' known over-generalization problem (§2, e.g., CESI conflating 'is brother of,' 'is son of,' 'is main villain of,' 'was professor of'); (4) a trained Schema Retriever (fine-tuned E5-mistral-7b-instruct via InfoNCE loss) that retrieves schema components relevant to the input text in a retrieval-augmented-generation-like manner during an iterative Refinement phase (§1, §3.2, Appendix A), which the authors list as a distinct contribution alongside the EDC framework itself.

protocol.description: EDC is a multi-call pipeline, not a single forward pass and not an interactive human-in-the-loop protocol in the sense of turn-by-turn external feedback. Each of the three phases (Extract, Define, Canonicalize) is a separate few-shot LLM prompting call, and the optional Refinement phase (EDC+R) re-runs the pipeline one or more additional times, feeding back the previous iteration's own extracted triplets and retrieved schema elements as a 'hint' in the next Extract call (§3.1, §3.2). This is best characterized as 'both': each individual phase call is single-shot few-shot prompting (no external verifier feedback within a phase), but the overall system loops across iterations using its own prior output as input, and Table 6 explicitly reports up to two such refinement iterations (EDC+2xR).
protocol.observation_content: Per call, the model sees: (1) OIE phase -- a few-shot prompt with example input text and demonstrated [Subject, Relation, Object] triplet extractions, then the actual input text to extract from (exact prompt template is embedded as a non-extractable inline figure in the source HTML per the extraction's Notes section; only the paper's own narration of its content and the worked example are available verbatim: input text 'Alan Shepard was born on Nov 18, 1923 and selected by NASA in 1959. He was a member of the Apollo 14 crew' -> triplets ['Alan Shepard','bornOn','Nov 18, 1923'], ['Alan Shepard','participatedIn','Apollo 14'], §3.1). (2) Schema Definition phase -- a prompt asking the LLM to produce a natural-language definition for each schema component (entity/relation type) induced by the extracted triplets (prompt figure also not rendered as text; example definitions given verbatim in prose: 'bornOn: The subject entity was born on the date specified by the object entity.' and 'participatedIn: The subject entity took part in the event or mission specified by the object entity.', §3.1). (3) Canonicalization phase -- given vector-similarity-retrieved candidate schema components (from the target schema, or from the growing self-built schema) plus their LLM-generated definitions, the model is asked whether/how to transform a triplet's relation into a canonical form (prompt figure not rendered; worked example transformation given: relations mapped to ['Alan Shepard','birthDate','Nov 18, 1923'], ['Alan Shepard','mission','Apollo 14'], §3.1). (4) In Refinement (EDC+R), the next iteration's Extract call additionally receives a 'hint' consisting of candidate entities (from the prior iteration's extraction plus fresh LLM entity extraction) and candidate relations (from the prior iteration plus the trained Schema Retriever's cosine-similarity retrieval over the schema) (§3.2). The exact verbatim prompt text for all three phase prompts is NOT recoverable from this extraction -- flagged in the source file as unrendered LaTeXML figure content (see Notes on figures/tables); unverified -- missing from extraction.

representations: unverified -- missing from extraction for controlled input/representation-format ablations. The paper does not report a comparison of alternative input serialization formats (e.g., JSON vs. plain text vs. table) for the triplets/schema fed to the LLM; the only 'representation' variable systematically varied is the choice of upstream OIE LLM (GPT-4 vs GPT-3.5-turbo vs Mistral-7b, §4.1, Tables 3-5) and presence/absence of the Schema Retriever's retrieved-relations hint (Table 1 ablation). No experiment isolates prompt formatting, key ordering, or serialization convention as an independent variable the way c11 would require.

models.name: GPT-4 (as OIE/extraction LLM; remaining framework components use GPT-3.5-turbo)
models.key_results: Best-performing LLM choice for OIE across all three datasets. WebNLG (Table 3): EDC Partial/Strict/Exact F1 = 0.783/0.733/0.756; EDC+R = 0.820/0.786/0.800 (vs. REGEN baseline F1 0.767/0.720/0.723). REBEL (Table 4): EDC F1 = 0.546/0.500/0.514; EDC+R = 0.601/0.559/0.574 (vs. GenIE baseline F1 0.385/0.356/0.364). Wiki-NRE (Table 5): EDC F1 = 0.683/0.677/0.678; EDC+R = 0.713/0.709/0.709 (vs. GenIE baseline F1 0.484/0.463/0.478). 'GPT-4 emerges as the top performer' (§4.2.1).

models.name: GPT-3.5-turbo (OIE LLM, and default LLM for all other framework components: Schema Definition, Canonicalization, hint construction)
strategies: iterative refinement (EDC+R, and EDC+2xR in Appendix B.2)
models.key_results: WebNLG (Table 3): EDC F1 = 0.746/0.688/0.713; EDC+R = 0.794/0.753/0.772. REBEL (Table 4): EDC F1 = 0.506/0.449/0.473; EDC+R = 0.559/0.516/0.529. Wiki-NRE (Table 5): EDC F1 = 0.647/0.638/0.640; EDC+R = 0.693/0.685/0.687. Ablation (Table 1, GPT-3.5-turbo only): removing Schema Retriever from the refinement hint (EDC+R w/o S.R.) drops WebNLG Partial F1 from 0.794 to 0.752, REBEL Partial F1 from 0.559 to 0.517, Wiki-NRE Partial F1 from 0.693 to 0.653 -- 'a notable decline in performance' (§4.2.1, Table 1). Extra refinement iteration (Table 6, Appendix B.2): EDC+2xR Partial F1 = 0.797 (WebNLG) / 0.564 (Rebel) / 0.697 (Wiki-NRE) vs. EDC+R 0.794/0.559/0.693 -- 'diminishing returns' (§Appendix B.2). Self-Canonicalization (Table 2, GPT-3.5-turbo for OIE): EDC precision/no.relations/redundancy = 0.956/200/0.833 (WebNLG), 0.867/225/0.831 (Rebel), 0.898/106/0.833 (Wiki-NRE), vs. CESI 0.724/280/0.893, 0.504/307/0.854, 0.753/114/0.849, vs. raw Open KG 0.982/529/0.927, 0.903/667/0.895, 0.909/204/0.881.

models.name: Mistral-7b (open-weight, locally-deployed OIE LLM)
models.key_results: WebNLG (Table 3): EDC F1 = 0.728/0.672/0.696; EDC+R = 0.762/0.720/0.739. REBEL (Table 4): EDC F1 = 0.516/0.453/0.483; EDC+R = 0.531/0.462/0.505. Wiki-NRE (Table 5): EDC F1 = 0.647/0.637/0.639; EDC+R = 0.663/0.649/0.658. 'Mistral-7b and GPT-3.5-turbo exhibiting comparable results' pre-refinement, but post-refinement 'the difference in performance between GPT-3.5-turbo and Mistral-7b is larger, suggesting Mistral-7b's was not as able to leverage the provided hints' (§4.2.1).

headline_numbers: EDC (GPT-4) beats REGEN baseline on WebNLG Partial F1: 0.783 vs 0.767 (Table 3); EDC+R (GPT-4): 0.820 (Table 3).
EDC (GPT-4) beats GenIE baseline on REBEL Partial F1 by a wide margin: 0.546 vs 0.385; EDC+R: 0.601 (Table 4).
EDC (GPT-4) beats GenIE baseline on Wiki-NRE Partial F1: 0.683 vs 0.484; EDC+R: 0.713 (Table 5).

Schema Retriever ablation (Table 1, GPT-3.5-turbo): removing retrieved relations from the refinement hint drops WebNLG Partial F1 from 0.794 to 0.752, REBEL from 0.559 to 0.517, Wiki-NRE from 0.693 to 0.653.

Self-Canonicalization precision (Table 2): EDC achieves 0.956/0.867/0.898 precision (WebNLG/Rebel/Wiki-NRE) with far fewer relation types than raw Open KG (200/225/106 vs 529/667/204) and lower redundancy scores than CESI (0.833/0.831/0.833 vs 0.893/0.854/0.849).
Schema Retriever recall@10 on held-out test splits: 0.823 (WebNLG), 0.663 (REBEL), 0.818 (Wiki-NRE) (Appendix A).
Human inter-annotator agreement for Self-Canonicalization manual evaluation: 0.94 (§4.2.2).

EDC extracts systematically more triplets per sentence than reference annotations on REBEL (+1.11 for GPT-4) and Wiki-NRE (+0.63), attributed to non-exhaustive reference annotations rather than model error (Table 7, §4.2.1, Appendix B.3).
failure_modes: The paper's main reported 'failure' is not model failure but scorer/dataset failure: EDC is 'penalized despite producing valid triplets' on REBEL and Wiki-NRE because their reference triplet sets are non-exhaustive (created via distant supervision alignment, unlike WebNLG which was authored directly from triplets) (§4.2.1, Appendix B.3). Worked examples: for 'Romany Love is a 1931 British musical film directed by Fred Paul and starring Esmond Knight, Florence McHugh and Roy Travers', EDC extracts three semantically-correct 'cast member' triplets but only one appears in the reference set; REBEL/Wiki-NRE references also contain triplets based on information extraneous to the source text (e.g., a 'country: Ethiopia' fact not stated in the given sentence about a footballer). On the LLM side, GenIE's constrained-generation baseline is reported to fail at extracting triplets containing literals such as numbers and dates, which is why the EDC-vs-baseline gap is larger on REBEL/Wiki-NRE than WebNLG (§4.2.1). For the self-canonicalization comparison, CESI (clustering baseline) is reported to over-generalize, incorrectly clustering distinct relations such as 'place of death,' 'place of birth,' 'date of death,' 'date of birth,' and 'cause of death' into a single category (§4.2.2). Mistral-7b is reported to benefit less from the refinement hint than GPT-3.5-turbo/GPT-4, i.e., a weaker model does not exploit the same context/feedback as effectively (§4.2.1). Diminishing returns are reported for further refinement iterations (Table 6, Appendix B.2).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper does not frame its results in terms of 'one forward pass vs. interaction loop' the way c11's design docs do, but the empirical pattern is directly analogous: each EDC phase call, run once (no refinement), already produces valid triplets and reasonable canonicalizations, but performance is 'consistently and significantly' improved by Refinement (EDC+R), i.e., re-running the pipeline with a hint constructed from the model's own prior output plus retrieved schema context (§4.2.1: 'Refinement (EDC+R) consistently and significantly enhances performance... a single refinement iteration with the hint improved performance for all the tested LLMs'). A second refinement iteration (EDC+2xR, Table 6, Appendix B.2) yields further but diminishing gains ('we observe diminishing returns and hence, only report one iteration in the main results'). This suggests the paper's implicit conclusion is that a single few-shot forward pass per phase gets most of the way there, but iterating the pipeline with self/retrieval-augmented context recovers additional accuracy with fast-decaying marginal returns -- there is no externally-supplied ground-truth feedback loop (no oracle correction between iterations), only the model's own prior extractions and a trained retriever's suggestions.

relation_to_candidate.position: EDC sits on a different point of the family's protocol space than c11 in several respects. Axis 1 (single-shot vs. interactive/multi-turn): EDC is a multi-call pipeline (Extract -> Define -> Canonicalize, optionally repeated with Refinement) where later calls consume the model's own prior outputs plus a trained retriever's suggestions -- not a single constrained forward pass, and not an interactive loop with an external verifier giving corrective feedback per turn (there is no oracle-in-the-loop; refinement is self-bootstrapped). c11 by contrast is explicitly framed as a single-shot, one-forward-pass task: one messy input value in, one canonical string out, scored by a single exact-match check. Axis 2 (what is observed): EDC's canonicalization phase observes vector-similarity-retrieved candidate schema components plus LLM-generated natural-language definitions of those components -- i.e., dynamically retrieved, model-generated side information -- whereas c11's canonicalizer must infer fixed invented house rules (key order, quoting, number/date formatting, affixes) either from a short rule statement or a small demo set, with no retrieval step and no schema-definition sub-phase. Axis 3 (output shape): EDC's output is a set/list of (subject, relation, object) triplets whose relation-phrase must match one of a schema's canonical labels (a classification-into-existing-or-newly-coined-label problem over an open/discovered schema) -- partial credit is possible and is in fact the paper's whole evaluation apparatus (Partial/Strict/Exact P/R/F1). c11's output is a single, fully-specified canonical string that must match a roughly 40-line reference function's output exactly, with strictly 0/1 whole-string scoring and no partial credit. Axis 4 (verifier type): EDC is scored by a token-based triplet-matching evaluation script (WebNLG eval script) for Target Alignment, plus manual/human annotation for Self-Canonicalization (precision, conciseness, redundancy via cosine similarity) -- a mixed automatic+human, partial-match verifier. c11 uses a fully deterministic, non-human, whole-string exact-match verifier against a fixed reference function, with no partial credit and no human judgment step.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c11: The overlap is at the level of the framing, not the mechanics: both tasks are ultimately about 'canonicalization' -- taking heterogeneous/redundant surface forms (relation phrases in EDC; JSON records, identifiers, phone/date strings in c11) and mapping them onto a single fixed canonical representation. Both papers explicitly motivate their canonicalization step by pointing to redundant/ambiguous surface variants that must be unified (EDC: 'profession'/'job'/'occupation' as semantically-equivalent relation phrases, §1; c11: canonical-JSON house rules and RFC 8785 JCS as the standard being deliberately deviated from). Both use exact/strict matching as one of their evaluation criteria (EDC's 'Strict' and 'Exact' P/R/F1 criteria, §4.1.1, are literal/exact matches at the triplet-element level, conceptually adjacent to c11's whole-string exact match, though EDC's is per-element/partial-creditable while c11's is all-or-nothing over the whole string).
relation_to_candidate.differences: EDC canonicalizes toward semantic equivalence classes (many surface relation phrases -> one meaning-preserving canonical label, chosen from/added to a schema, verified by LLM judgment of semantic equivalence) whereas c11 canonicalizes toward syntactic/formatting rules that are arbitrary and invented (key sort order, quote style, whitespace, number formatting, affixes) with no semantic content and a single deterministic ground truth defined by a reference function, not by LLM judgment. EDC's schema of canonical labels is discovered/built by the system itself (Self Canonicalization) or matched against an existing large target schema via embedding retrieval; c11's 'house rules' are fixed and given (or must be inferred from a handful of examples), with no retrieval or discovery process at all. EDC requires and reports on a trained component (Schema Retriever, fine-tuned E5-mistral-7b-instruct); c11 as designed is entirely inference-time/prompted, with no fine-tuning. EDC's difficulty dial is schema size/dataset scale (159/200/45 relation types across WebNLG/REBEL/Wiki-NRE, §4.1); c11's difficulty dial is the number and type of invented house rules per instance/stratum (5-6 rules, with computation-heavy rules like check digits flagged as separately hard). EDC's evaluation tolerates partial correctness and non-exhaustive/noisy references (a major discussed limitation, §4.2.1/Appendix B.3); c11 by design uses a clean, contamination-proof, exhaustively-specified reference function with no annotation noise.
relation_to_candidate.what_c11_would_add: c11 would add a genuinely single-forward-pass, whole-string, zero-partial-credit protocol that isolates pure rule-following/formatting precision, decoupled from any semantic-equivalence judgment, retrieval, or multi-call refinement loop -- none of which EDC's design tests. Where EDC's 'canonicalization' capability is entangled with open information extraction quality, schema discovery, and retrieval-augmented iteration (so its reported numbers reflect a compound pipeline's accuracy, not a clean single-call formatting-rule-following measurement), c11 would isolate that specific latent skill (can a model reproduce N independent, deliberately non-standard formatting rules exactly, in one pass, against a fixed byte-exact oracle) with a contamination-proof synthetic generator and a scoring regime that gives no credit for near-misses. c11 would also add a genuinely adversarial construction relative to public conventions (rules chosen to actively diverge from RFC 8785 JCS / ISO dates / standard slugify), whereas EDC's canonical schema is either the dataset's own natural target schema or a self-discovered one, never adversarially designed to trip up standard-convention priors.

corpus_claim: EDC / Extract, Define, Canonicalize (Zhang & Soh, EMNLP 2024) is a canonicalize-step reference for the domain; primarily c12's parent, but it anchors the canonicalization framing c11 shares.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The paper does center a 'Canonicalize' phase/step (Phase 3 of EDC, and the entire self-canonicalization evaluation in §4.2.2/Table 2), so 'canonicalize-step reference for the domain' and 'anchors the canonicalization framing' are accurate at the level of shared vocabulary and general problem framing (unifying redundant/ambiguous surface forms into a single canonical representation). However, EDC's canonicalization target is semantic-equivalence-based (unifying relation phrases like 'profession'/'job'/'occupation' into one meaning class, verified by LLM judgment and embedding similarity against a discovered or pre-defined schema, §2-§3), not syntactic/formatting-convention-based (key order, quoting, whitespace, number/date format, affix rules) the way c11's house-convention canonicalization is. There is no mention in the paper of JSON canonicalization, RFC 8785/JCS, slugification, or any of c11's specific house-rule families (key sorting, escape/quote policy, number formatting). The connection is thus real but at the level of shared high-level framing ('canonicalization' as a named step/problem) rather than shared task mechanics, output type, or evaluation method -- consistent with the claim's own qualifier that EDC is 'primarily c12's parent' (a schema/extraction-focused sibling candidate) and only secondarily relevant to c11.
baseline_relevant: Dataset scale/difficulty dials used: WebNLG test split (1165 text-triplet pairs, 159 relation types), REBEL sampled subset (1000 pairs from 105,516, 200 relation types), Wiki-NRE sampled subset (1000 pairs from 29,619, 45 relation types) (§4.1) -- illustrates how a canonicalization-adjacent benchmark scales difficulty via schema/label-space size, a dial c11 could analogously use (number of invented house rules / table sizes) rather than dataset size.

Evaluation used three tiers of matching strictness -- Exact (full match ignoring element type), Partial (allows partial triplet match), Strict (exact match including element types) (§4.1.1) -- all partial-credit-capable, in contrast to c11's required 0/1 whole-string exact match; useful contrast baseline for justifying why c11 should NOT use partial credit if isolating rule-following precision is the goal.

Manual/human evaluation was necessary for the no-target-schema (self-canonicalization) setting because 'canonicalized triplets may use relations phrased differently from the reference triplets,' making token-based scoring unsuitable (§4.1.1) -- reinforces that c11's decision to use a fixed reference-function oracle (not human judgment, not fuzzy matching) is what makes automatic, scalable, contamination-proof scoring possible for a canonicalization task.

Floors/ceilings observed: EDC (best config, GPT-4 + EDC+R) reaches Partial F1 up to 0.820 (WebNLG), 0.601 (REBEL), 0.713 (Wiki-NRE) (Tables 3-5) -- none near a ceiling of 1.0, and the paper attributes much of the shortfall to annotation/reference-set noise rather than model error (§4.2.1, Appendix B.3), a caution relevant to baseline design: don't assume gap-to-ceiling reflects true model failure without checking oracle/reference quality.

Ablation methodology: Table 1 isolates one component (the Schema Retriever) by removing it from the refinement hint and re-measuring F1 across all three datasets and all three matching criteria with a fixed LLM (GPT-3.5-turbo) -- a clean single-factor ablation template c11 could mirror when isolating individual house rules' contribution to whole-pool accuracy.

Contamination/data-release status: full source code and experiment-replication materials are released at https://github.com/clear-nus/edc (§4, first paragraph) under presumed open license; datasets used (WebNLG+2020 v3.0, REBEL, Wiki-NRE) are pre-existing public benchmarks, so contamination risk exists for EDC's evaluation sets in a way c11's synthetic/regenerable pool is explicitly designed to avoid.

Diminishing returns from iteration: a second refinement pass (EDC+2xR) improves Partial F1 only marginally over one pass (e.g., WebNLG 0.797 vs 0.794, REBEL 0.564 vs 0.559, Wiki-NRE 0.697 vs 0.693; Table 6) -- relevant baseline signal that repeated self-refinement/iteration in a pipeline task saturates quickly, useful context if considering whether c11 should test single-pass vs. iterated variants.

Extraction-quality caveat carried over from the source file: the exact verbatim text of all three in-context few-shot prompts (OIE, Schema Definition, Canonicalization) is NOT recoverable -- these rendered as non-text LaTeXML figures in the v1 HTML and are marked 'unrendered' in the extraction; any claim about exact prompt wording/format beyond the paper's own narrated paraphrase is unverified -- missing from extraction.
quotes: "To address this problem, we propose a three-phase framework named Extract-Define-Canonicalize (EDC): open information extraction followed by schema definition and post-hoc canonicalization." (Abstract)

"However, these methods are currently limited to small and domain-specific scenarios -- to ensure the validity of generated triplets, schema information (e.g., possible entity and relation types) has to be included in the prompt." (§1 Introduction)

"Compared to the existing canonicalization methods, EDC is more general; it works whether a target schema is provided or not... Furthermore, by allowing the LLMs to verify if a transformation can be performed (instead of solely relying on the embedding similarity), EDC alleviates the over-generalization issue faced by previous methods." (§2 Background)
"The results in Table 1 show that ablating the Schema Retriever leads to a notable decline in performance." (§4.2.1 Target Alignment)
"We observed a high inter-annotator agreement score of 0.94." (§4.2.2 Self Canonicalization)

"EDC accurately canonicalizes the open KG to yield a schema that is both more concise and less redundant compared to CESI... we observed CESI inappropriately clusters diverse relations such as 'place of death,' 'place of birth,' 'date of death,' 'date of birth,' and 'cause of death' into a single 'date of death' category." (§4.2.2 Self Canonicalization)
"Refinement (EDC+R) consistently and significantly enhances performance... a single refinement iteration with the hint improved performance for all the tested LLMs." (§4.2.1 Target Alignment)

"Although further refinement improves the results in a stable manner, we observe diminishing returns and hence, only report one iteration in the main results." (Appendix B.2 Effect of more refinement iterations)

corrections: models[1] (GPT-3.5-turbo entry) key_results: Wiki-NRE (Table 5) EDC+R Exact F1 was given as 0.657, which is actually Table 1's ablation-summary value for a different table; the source Table 5 row for EDC+R/GPT-3.5 is Partial/Strict/Exact F1 = 0.693/0.685/0.687. Corrected 0.657 -> 0.687 (classic cross-table digit substitution; Table 1 and Table 5 report slightly different Wiki-NRE numbers for what should be the same run, a genuine minor inconsistency in the source paper itself, but the dossier text explicitly cited 'Table 5' so it must match Table 5's own value).
